responses: We built an AI system for automated root cause analysis in a distributed microservices platform with 140 services. When a service degradation or outage is detected, the system ingests log streams, distributed traces, and metric time series from the affected service and its upstream and downstream dependencies, and produces a ranked list of probable root causes with supporting evidence. The problem being solved is mean-time-to-diagnosis: our on-call engineers were spending an average of 47 minutes diagnosing root causes before they could begin remediation. Post-deployment over 10 months, MTTD has dropped to 11 minutes for incidents where the root cause falls within the system's learned pattern library. Incidents with novel causes still require manual diagnosis but now start with a structured evidence summary, reducing average diagnosis time for those cases from 47 minutes to 23 minutes.

We evaluated four approaches: a rule-based correlation engine using pre-defined service dependency graphs; a supervised anomaly detection model trained on historical incident data; an unsupervised clustering approach that would group similar incident signatures; and an LLM-based analysis pipeline that reads log excerpts and traces and produces narrative root cause hypotheses. We rejected the pure rule-based engine because our service topology changes weekly — new services, modified dependencies — and maintaining rules at that velocity was not feasible. The supervised model required labeled incident data with confirmed root causes, which we had for only 340 incidents, not enough to cover the distribution of failure modes. The unsupervised clustering produced useful groupings but could not explain why a cluster corresponded to a particular root cause. We chose a hybrid: unsupervised clustering for pattern recognition plus the LLM for evidence synthesis and explanation. The explicit thing we gave up: the LLM component cannot be independently audited for correctness — when it produces a confident-sounding root cause explanation that is wrong, there is no structural check that catches it before the engineer reads it.

We identified five failure modes with mitigations and monitoring procedures. First: false root cause leading engineer to remediate the wrong component, extending the incident. Mitigation: the system displays confidence scores and the specific evidence used for each candidate root cause; engineers are trained that low-confidence outputs require independent verification. Recovery: we track remediation success rate per root cause type; when an on-call session ends with an incorrect root cause, it is flagged for inclusion in the next model calibration round. Second: log ingestion lag causing analysis to run on incomplete data, producing premature root cause hypotheses during fast-moving incidents. Mitigation: the system displays a data freshness indicator and delays analysis publication by 90 seconds after incident detection to allow data propagation. Third: novel failure modes not in the pattern library producing confident but incorrect outputs because the model applies the closest known pattern. Mitigation: novelty detection flags when the incident signature has low similarity to any known pattern; novel-flagged incidents are routed to senior on-call by default. Fourth: the system becomes a crutch — engineers stop building diagnosis intuition because they rely on the AI output. Mitigation: we conduct quarterly incident post-mortems without AI output visible to maintain diagnostic skills. Fifth: LLM provider outage during an incident, removing the synthesis capability. Mitigation: the clustering component produces a structured evidence summary independently; this is what engineers fall back to.

The most significant learning was about the difference between retrieval accuracy and diagnostic accuracy. In month 2, we discovered that the system was retrieving highly relevant log excerpts and assembling them into compelling root cause narratives that were wrong — the evidence was real but the causal chain the LLM inferred was incorrect. We had been evaluating the system on evidence retrieval quality and log ranking, not on whether the narrative conclusion was correct. We added a human verification protocol for the first 6 weeks of any new model version, where on-call engineers record whether the stated root cause was confirmed in post-incident review. This process revealed a 15% narrative error rate that retrieval metrics had not surfaced. We now treat narrative accuracy as the primary evaluation metric and retrieval metrics as a secondary diagnostic. The AI also confidently produced root cause narratives for a category of incidents where it was reasoning from incomplete traces — it had inferred causation from correlation across services without enough data to establish the dependency. We added a minimum evidence threshold that suppresses narrative publication when fewer than 3 corroborating signals are present, which reduced confident-but-wrong outputs by 60%.

rationale: L4 with strong tradeoff articulation (earns 5: four alternatives evaluated with substantive tradeoffs and explicit naming of what the chosen approach gives up). Clarity earns 4 — specific outcome with two distinct measurement categories. Boundary stays 3 — novel causes and scope limitations are mentioned but not precisely defined. Blast radius scores 3 — consequences of wrong root cause are clear but no progressive containment strategy. Override evidence earns 3 — specific AI errors caught and mitigated, not yet a systematic framework.
